title: Round Springfield

jokes.text: His sexual-harassment suit.
jokes.setup: Krusty's 'expensive new suit' suggests clothing
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's a legal lawsuit, not clothing
jokes.explanation: Clean misdirection with wordplay - economical and well-structured

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Hey, check out my expensive new suit!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, that's a nice suit!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Suit? This is a lawsuit! I'm being sued by seventeen people!

jokes.text: You're hot. Let's get some dinner after the show. / I have dinner with all my employees. Right, Sideshow Mel? / We've never spoken outside of work.
jokes.setup: Krusty tries to deflect harassment by claiming he's friendly with all employees
jokes.punchline: Mel immediately contradicts him with deadpan honesty
jokes.explanation: Perfect three-part structure with Mel's deadpan delivery undercutting Krusty

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: I'm friendly with all my employees. Ask anyone here.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Mel
dialogue.line: He once made me eat a live chicken.

jokes.text: Wasn't my fault. It was the Percodan. / If you ask me, that stuff rots your brain. / And now a word from my new sponsor. / Percodan?! Oh, crap!
jokes.setup: Krusty blames Percodan then immediately criticizes it
jokes.punchline: Discovers his sponsor is the same drug he just disparaged
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating irony perfectly suited to Krusty's hypocritical character

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: This show is brought to you by Percodan. Now, Percodan is a habit-forming drug. Don't use it.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Well, we're back, and I'm feeling great! Must be the Percodan.

jokes.text: The guy who chopped up George Washington?
jokes.setup: Question about George Washington Carver
jokes.punchline: Bart confuses him with a murderer who dismembered George Washington
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart ignorance - absurd but character-consistent

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, isn't George Washington Carver the guy who like, chopped up George Washington?

jokes.text: Like when a sign says, 'Do not feed the bears'... man, you'd better not feed the bears.
jokes.setup: Homer claims to have learned wisdom with age
jokes.punchline: His 'wisdom' is just stating the obvious about bear signs
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer pseudo-wisdom - perfectly captures his confident stupidity

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, I've learned a lot of wisdom with age.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: For example, if you see a sign that says 'Beware of Dog', it means there's a dog.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And if it says 'Beware of Bear', there's probably a bear.

jokes.text: 'No teacher shall be held accountable if Bart Simpson dies.' / We're also absolved if Milhouse gets eaten by the school snake.
jokes.setup: School charter specifically exempts Bart's death
jokes.punchline: Escalates to Milhouse being eaten by school snake
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation with great specificity - implies these deaths are common enough to require legal protection

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Krabappel
dialogue.line: Well, according to our charter, Bart's death is specifically exempted from liability.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: What about Milhouse?

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Krabappel
dialogue.line: Oh, he's fair game.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: And that's how Milhouse was eaten by the school snake.

jokes.text: Hey, cool, there's a rabbit in here.
jokes.setup: Just mentioned Milhouse might be eaten by school snake
jokes.punchline: Milhouse casually discovers something in snake habitat
jokes.explanation: Perfect oblivious Milhouse moment - creates visual of him about to fulfill the prophecy

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Hey, there's a whole bunch of money in here!

jokes.text: Budget cuts. They've even got Groundskeeper Willie teaching French.
jokes.setup: Explanation for why lunch lady is now nurse
jokes.punchline: Even more absurd job reassignment mentioned
jokes.explanation: Good setup for next joke. Escalates absurdity of budget cuts with perfect character choice.

jokes.text: Bonjour, you cheese-eating surrender monkeys.
jokes.setup: Willie teaching French due to budget cuts
jokes.punchline: Insults French people while teaching their language
jokes.explanation: Iconic line that became cultural touchstone - perfectly captures Willie's aggressive personality while teaching

dialogue.speaker: Groundskeeper Willie
dialogue.line: Bonjour, class. Today we're learning French, the language of surrender and baguettes. Now listen here, ye French are a bunch of lazy, beret-wearing cowards who couldn't fight their way out of a paper bag if it had a white flag in it. But ye need to learn yer own language, so repeat after me: 'Je suis ridicule.'

jokes.text: Your choice: Manic-Depressive Mouse or the Bluebird of Unhappiness.
jokes.setup: Offering chewable Prozac for kids
jokes.punchline: Cartoon mascots with depression themes
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark satire of children's medication marketing with twisted mascot names

dialogue.speaker: Lunchlady Doris
dialogue.line: We got Prozac chewables for the kids now. Grape flavor!

dialogue.speaker: Lunchlady Doris
dialogue.line: We got Sad Sack the Depressed Donkey, Suicidal Sam the Seahorse, and Prozac Pete the Pelican.

dialogue.speaker: Lunchlady Doris
dialogue.line: Kids love 'em. They're collectible!

jokes.text: Oh, look, here comes Lumpy, the school snake. / Help! Help! / Oh, Lord.
jokes.setup: Skinner deflects from unconscious Bart by pointing out snake
jokes.punchline: Milhouse immediately gets attacked, confirming the earlier prophecy
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback execution - the exact scenario mentioned earlier immediately comes true

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Look! A snake!

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Where?!

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Ahhhhh!

jokes.text: Well, in many cases, the floor is the best... Oh, look, here comes Lumpy, the school snake.
jokes.setup: Skinner tries to rationalize student on floor
jokes.punchline: Immediately distracted by approaching dangerous snake
jokes.explanation: Good callback that escalates earlier snake jokes. Skinner's bureaucratic rationalization interrupted by actual crisis.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Now, now, there's a perfectly logical explanation for why this student is on the floor.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: He's simply taking a well-deserved rest after—Oh my God, a snake!

jokes.text: This boy's appendix is inflamed and about to burst... which will make it easier to find once I get in there.
jokes.setup: Medical diagnosis of serious condition
jokes.punchline: Casual admission that he doesn't know where organs are located
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dr. Hibbert moment - cheerfully incompetent medical advice

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, I'm afraid I have some bad news. You have a serious condition affecting your... uh... (gestures vaguely) ...one of the organs in there somewhere.

dialogue.speaker: Patient
dialogue.line: Doctor, which organ exactly?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: That's just it — I haven't the faintest idea where they actually are. I just tell people what they want to hear and charge them $200.

jokes.text: Homer, this is an operation. / Hot dogs! Get your hot dogs here!
jokes.setup: Homer wants hot dog during surgery
jokes.punchline: Hot dog vendor appears in operating room
jokes.explanation: Absurd escalation of Homer's appetite - vendor materializes in impossible location

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I want a hot dog.

dialogue.speaker: Hot dog vendor
dialogue.line: Hot dog! Get your hot dog here!

jokes.text: Maybe if I fiddle with these knobs. / Hey, I smell gas. / Pleasant gas. / Night-night gas.
jokes.setup: Homer randomly adjusting medical equipment
jokes.punchline: Accidentally releases anesthesia and talks himself to sleep
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer sequence - destructive curiosity leading to self-inflicted consequence

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why is there a button labeled 'DO NOT PRESS'? That's like, asking for it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Zzzzz... wait, why do I suddenly feel so... so sleepy... and floaty... Mmm, floaty donuts...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Did I just... did I just knock myself out? That's actually... pretty smart... no more... problems... zzzzz...

jokes.text: A jagged, metal cereal O.
jokes.setup: Mysterious cause of Bart's appendicitis
jokes.punchline: Absurdly dangerous breakfast cereal piece
jokes.explanation: Sets up the Krusty cereal lawsuit plot with absurd product safety failure

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, I've found the cause of your son's appendicitis.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What is it, Doc?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: A toy prize from a box of Krusty-O's cereal. It appears to be a small propeller.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: A propeller? In a cereal box?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: I'm afraid so. And it was still spinning when we removed it.

jokes.text: Dr. Hibbert, can I have my appendix out? / Why not. Follow me, kids. / Nurse, prep these children.
jokes.setup: Lisa asks about getting surgery like Bart
jokes.punchline: Dr. Hibbert casually agrees to unnecessary surgery on children
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation of Dr. Hibbert's casual medical malpractice

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dr. Hibbert, can I get surgery like Bart?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose we could operate. Nothing serious, just something to keep you in the hospital for a few weeks. How about we remove your appendix? Everyone's got two of those anyway.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But I only have one appendix.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Eh, you won't miss it. Besides, the hospital cafeteria has a surprisingly good meatloaf on Tuesdays.

jokes.text: How'd you get a name like that? / Let me put it this way. You ever been to the dentist? / Yeah. / Not me.
jokes.setup: Lisa asks about his unusual name
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's never been to dentist, explaining bleeding gums
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/payoff explaining the literal meaning behind the blues name

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Why do they call you Bleeding Gums Murphy?

dialogue.speaker: Bleeding Gums Murphy
dialogue.line: Well, it all started when I was a young man. I never had the money for a dentist, so my gums started bleeding, and they never stopped.

jokes.text: Hello, I'm Dr. Cheeks. / I'm doing my rounds, and I'm a little behind.
jokes.setup: Doctor introduces himself as Dr. Cheeks
jokes.punchline: Says he's 'behind' on rounds (butt pun)
jokes.explanation: Weak pun that relies on obvious wordplay without much craft

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Cheeks
dialogue.line: I'm afraid I'm a bit behind on my rounds.

jokes.text: He used to laugh at the most inappropriate times. / I've got an older brother that I'll never see. / He's a jazz musician or some such. Oh, well. Bye-bye.
jokes.setup: Bleeding Gums mentions brother who laughed inappropriately
jokes.punchline: Dr. Hibbert dismissively mentions jazz musician brother while laughing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant ironic reveal - they're brothers but don't recognize each other, and Hibbert laughs inappropriately as described

dialogue.speaker: Bleeding Gums Murphy
dialogue.line: My brother, he laughed at the wrong time.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, you know, my brother's a jazz musician. Ha ha!

jokes.text: This isn't a saxophone, it's an umbrella. / So I've played a umbrella for 30 years? / Why didn't anybody tell me? / We all thought it was funny.
jokes.setup: Blind musician giving away his 'saxophone'
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's been playing umbrella for decades while people watched
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about exploitation of blind musician - reveals cruelty of audience

dialogue.speaker: Blind Willy Witherspoon
dialogue.line: Well, I'm givin' away my saxophone to one of you young folks.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Wow, really? Thank you!

dialogue.speaker: Blind Willy Witherspoon
dialogue.line: Yep, I've been playin' this thing for decades while crowds gathered 'round watchin' me play.

dialogue.speaker: Blind Willy Witherspoon
dialogue.line: But I gotta tell ya... it ain't been no saxophone.

dialogue.speaker: Blind Willy Witherspoon
dialogue.line: It's been an umbrella.

jokes.text: How to Make Love to Steve Allen. From the author of Happiness Is a Naked Steve Allen... Journey to the Center of Steve Allen and The Joy of Cooking Steve Allen.
jokes.setup: Announcing Steve Allen's book
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd titles all about Steve Allen
jokes.explanation: Good escalation parody of celebrity book titles. The specificity and absurdity build nicely.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: "Steve Allen: The Man Who Smiled Too Much."

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: "America's Wackiest Wino."

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: "Snoring: I Do It."

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: "The Big Book of Marginal Talent."

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: "They Kept Laughing, But Why?"

jokes.text: I cut my first and only album, Sax on the Beach.
jokes.setup: Talking about his jazz album
jokes.punchline: Album title parodies 'Sex on the Beach' cocktail
jokes.explanation: Straightforward pun on cocktail name - functional but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Bleeding Gums Murphy
dialogue.line: My new album is called 'Sax on the Beach.'

jokes.text: I'd like another Fabergé egg, please. / Sir, don't you think you've had enough? / I'll tell you when I've had enough!
jokes.setup: $1500/day 'habit' suggests drugs
jokes.punchline: He's addicted to expensive collectible eggs instead
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - subverts drug addiction trope with absurd luxury addiction

dialogue.speaker: Bleeding Gums Murphy
dialogue.line: Man, I got a $1500 a day habit.

dialogue.speaker: Shopkeeper
dialogue.line: Drugs?

dialogue.speaker: Bleeding Gums Murphy
dialogue.line: Worse. Collectible eggs.

jokes.text: Hey, kids. Meet Grandpa Murphy. / But we have three grandpas already. / This one is a great jazz musician. / They all are.
jokes.setup: Cosby introducing jazz musician grandfather
jokes.punchline: Kids reveal all their grandfathers are already jazz musicians
jokes.explanation: Smart satire of Cosby Show's idealized family dynamics and jazz obsession

dialogue.speaker: Bill Cosby
dialogue.line: Well children, let me tell you about my grandfather, a great jazz musician.

dialogue.speaker: Cosby Kids
dialogue.line: But Mr. Cosby, all of our grandfathers are already jazz musicians.

jokes.text: You see, the kids, they listen to the rap music... which gives them the brain damage. With their hipping and the hopping and the bipping and the bopping.
jokes.setup: Cosby explaining why kids don't appreciate jazz
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous made-up words to describe hip-hop
jokes.explanation: Perfect Cosby impression with nonsense words - captures his speech patterns

dialogue.speaker: Bill Cosby
dialogue.line: Kids today don't appreciate jazz. They listen to that hip-hop with all those ridiculous made-up words like 'fo' shizzle' and 'bling-bling' and 'da bomb.'

jokes.text: You see, jazz is like a Jell-O Pudding Pop. / No. Actually, it's more like Kodak film. / No. Actually, jazz is like the New Coke. / It'll be around forever.
jokes.setup: Cosby trying to explain jazz with analogies
jokes.punchline: Compares it to New Coke, which famously failed, while claiming permanence
jokes.explanation: Escalating bad analogies ending with perfect ironic failure - New Coke was discontinued

jokes.text: I contend the tourists were decapitated... before they entered the Krustyland House of Knives.
jokes.setup: Krusty defending his theme park's safety
jokes.punchline: Absurd legal defense that visitors were already dead
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd legal defense - implies a 'House of Knives' is somehow normal

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Your Honor, I move to dismiss this case. My lawyers have evidence that all the victims were already dead before they entered Krusty Land.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: That's preposterous! How could they possibly be dead?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: They were from out of state.

jokes.text: Oh, boy! This thing is shredding my insides! / Krusty, that wasn't the metal one. That was a regular Krusty-O. / It's poison.
jokes.setup: Krusty eats cereal to prove it's safe
jokes.punchline: Regular cereal is even more dangerous than the metal one
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - reveals the 'normal' product is even worse than the defective one

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: See? I'm eating this cereal and I'm fine. Completely safe!

dialogue.speaker: Aide
dialogue.line: Mr. Krusty, that's the metal cereal. The regular cereal is even more dangerous.

jokes.text: I stopped brushing my teeth so I can play just like you.
jokes.setup: Lisa wants to emulate Bleeding Gums Murphy
jokes.punchline: She's imitating his poor dental hygiene for musical reasons
jokes.explanation: Sweet but misguided Lisa moment - she misunderstands what makes music good

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I want to emulate Bleeding Gums Murphy.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: But Lisa, he has terrible dental hygiene.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I know, but it's for musical reasons.

jokes.text: Barney, this is a five-minute chip. It's worth a Pabst.
jokes.setup: Barney trading A.A. chip at bar
jokes.punchline: Five minutes of sobriety only worth cheap beer
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about alcoholism with perfect character integration for both Moe and Barney

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: What are you doing with that A.A. chip, Barney?

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Trading it in. Five minutes of sobriety ain't worth much.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: What'll you take for it?

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Cheap beer.

jokes.text: And with a flute up his nose, Ralph Wiggum.
jokes.setup: Introducing orchestra members
jokes.punchline: Ralph is playing flute incorrectly through his nose
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph moment - completely wrong but delivered with innocent confidence

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: And here we have Ralph Wiggum on the flute.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I'm playing it with my nose!

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Yes, well, that's... not quite the traditional method.

jokes.text: Hopefully not forever.
jokes.setup: Introducing 'Stars and Stripes Forever'
jokes.punchline: Undermining the patriotic song title
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan delivery that subverts patriotic expectations. Efficient but not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: And now, our school band will play 'Stars and Stripes Forever'.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: My dad says the only forever thing is debt.

jokes.text: This sucks.
jokes.setup: School orchestra performance begins
jokes.punchline: Immediate honest assessment from audience
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective audience reaction - cuts through any pretense

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: My band-aid has a band-aid.

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: That was the worst concert I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Yeah, and I've heard plenty of bad music. I AM bad music.

jokes.text: Well, it's like the time that your cat, Snowball, got run over. / Remember, honey? / Yeah. / What I'm saying is, all we have to do... is go down to the pound and get a new jazzman.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to comfort Lisa about death
jokes.punchline: Suggests replacing dead musician like a pet
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - treats unique human relationships like replaceable objects

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, don't worry, sweetheart. When a pet dies, you just get a new one.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But Dad, we're talking about a musician, not a pet.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, and...?

jokes.text: If it helps, I believe that after you die... you can come back as whatever you want. / I'll be a butterfly. / How come? / Because nobody ever suspects the butterfly.
jokes.setup: Bart offers comfort about reincarnation
jokes.punchline: Reveals sinister plan to be unsuspected criminal butterfly
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from sweet comfort to criminal scheming - perfectly Bart

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Don't worry, Lisa. When you die, you'll be reincarnated as something better.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Really? Like what?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: A butterfly. They're beautiful, free, and nobody suspects them of crimes.

jokes.text: I didn't burn down the school. / It was the butterfly, I tell you. The butterfly! / He's crazy, boys. Get the Taser.
jokes.setup: Ralph being arrested
jokes.punchline: Blames butterfly, unknowingly referencing Bart's plan
jokes.explanation: Immediate callback showing Bart's plan already working - Ralph blamed butterfly

dialogue.speaker: Police Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Ralph, you're under arrest.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: But I didn't do it! It was a butterfly! A big orange and black butterfly!

dialogue.speaker: Police Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Son, that's the worst alibi I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I know! It was so pretty though.

jokes.text: There it is. Death! / It's only Maggie. / Oh, yeah. / Death! / That's only the cat. / Death! / That's Maggie again, Grampa.
jokes.setup: Grampa seeing death everywhere
jokes.punchline: Keeps misidentifying harmless family members and pets
jokes.explanation: Good running gag building with repetition - shows Grampa's paranoid confusion

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I see the grim reaper! He's coming for me!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Grampa, that's just Dad in a black robe.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Death has taken the form of a balding man!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Grampa, look — it's Santa's Little Helper.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: The hound of hell itself! I'm doomed!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's our dog.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: And there — the angel of death in a yellow dress!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Grampa, I'm your granddaughter.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Why does the reaper keep visiting? I'm not ready!

jokes.text: 'Starland Vocal Band'? They suck! / Death!
jokes.setup: Grampa looking at tattoo recommendation
jokes.punchline: Harshly judges obscure 70s band then sees death again
jokes.explanation: Specific cultural reference with Grampa's blunt assessment - continues death running gag

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: What's this? A tattoo of some obscure 70s band? That's the worst idea I've ever heard. Back in my day, we didn't need no fancy tattoos to look cool. We just had our natural charm and good looks.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... I recognize that logo. That's... that's the Grim Reaper! Oh geez, not again!

jokes.text: No! It was Bleeding Gums Murphy. / Anyway, Bloody Gums Murphy was quite the sousaphone player... / Saxophone! He was a jazz musician.
jokes.setup: Reverend getting the name wrong
jokes.punchline: Gets both name and instrument wrong despite correction
jokes.explanation: Shows Lovejoy's disconnection and Lisa's frustration with authority figures

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Lisa Simpson, wonderful to see you. I hear you play the oboe.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Actually, it's the saxophone.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, the oboe. Lovely instrument.

jokes.text: What do you do, follow my husband around? / Lady, he's putting my kids through college.
jokes.setup: Marge annoyed vendor appears everywhere Homer goes
jokes.punchline: Vendor explains Homer is his best customer
jokes.explanation: Perfect economics joke - Homer's appetite is supporting a family business

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Why does that hot dog vendor keep following us everywhere?

dialogue.speaker: Hot Dog Vendor
dialogue.line: Following? Lady, I'm not following your husband. He's my best customer! I just go where the money is.

jokes.text: Albert Dershman, who can hold three billiard balls in his mouth.
jokes.setup: Listing qualified legal team members
jokes.punchline: Lawyer qualified by irrelevant party trick
jokes.explanation: Absurd legal credentials - suggests incompetent legal system

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: I'm qualified because I can pull a rabbit out of a hat.

jokes.text: Five hundred dollars on red. / The winner is black. / Cool.
jokes.setup: Bart gambling his settlement money
jokes.punchline: Immediately loses everything but stays positive
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart reaction - finds losing all his money 'cool' somehow

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, I've lost everything, but you know what? I'm still smiling!

dialogue.speaker: Casino worker
dialogue.line: Kid, you just lost a million dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, but at least I got to experience the thrill of gambling. That's worth something, right?

jokes.text: Jazz. They just make it up as they go along. I could do that. / That's 'Mary Had a Little Lamb.' / Okay, then this: / That's the same thing. You just replaced 'dees' with 'do's.'
jokes.setup: Homer dismisses jazz as easy improvisation
jokes.punchline: His 'improvisation' is just children's song with different syllables
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer musical ignorance - thinks minimal changes equal creativity

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Jazz is just easy improvisation. Anyone can do it.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that's not true. Jazz requires real skill and training.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh please, Lisa. Watch this. Doo-bee-doo-bee-doo, skee-bop-a-dop-a-dop...

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that's just 'Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star' with different syllables.

jokes.text: Our broadcast range is only 23 feet... which makes us the most powerful jazz station in the entire U.S. Of A.
jokes.setup: Radio station admitting tiny broadcast range
jokes.punchline: Claims this makes them most powerful jazz station nationally
jokes.explanation: Brilliant backhanded commentary on jazz's commercial decline - tiny range still makes them #1

dialogue.speaker: DJ
dialogue.line: Yeah, we're a low-power station with a pretty tiny broadcast range.

dialogue.speaker: DJ
dialogue.line: But that makes us the most powerful jazz station in the nation!

jokes.text: Hello. This is 'Moleman in the Morning.' Good Moleman to you. / Today, part four of our series of the agonizing pain... in which I live every day.
jokes.setup: Morning radio show greeting
jokes.punchline: Cheerful introduction to series about personal agony
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moleman character moment - upbeat delivery of deeply depressing content

dialogue.speaker: Hans Moleman
dialogue.line: Good morning, I'm your host Hans Moleman. Welcome to 'Pain and Suffering,' the show where we explore the deepest agonies of the human experience. Stick around!

jokes.text: Behold the ultimate Pog.
jokes.setup: Bart looking to spend $500
jokes.punchline: Comic Book Guy offers overpriced 90s fad toy
jokes.explanation: Good period satire of 90s collectible fads and Comic Book Guy's exploitation

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I've got $500 to spend. What's the coolest thing you've got?

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: Ah, a customer with disposable income. Behold! A mint-condition Radioactive Man #1 from 1991. Only $499.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What about that?

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: That? That's a Beanie Baby. It's worth at least $5,000. I'll let it go for $500.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wouldn't that be a rip-off?

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: Worst. Negotiations. Ever.

jokes.text: You must avenge my death, Kimba. I mean, Simba. / Luke, I am your father. / This is CNN.
jokes.setup: Bleeding Gums appearing as ghost
jokes.punchline: Other famous cloud appearances interrupt with their catchphrases
jokes.explanation: Meta-joke about cloud ghost trope in movies - CNN inclusion is absurd escalation

dialogue.speaker: Bleeding Gums Murphy
dialogue.line: Lisa, it's me, Bleeding Gums Murphy. I've come back from heaven to—

dialogue.speaker: Mufasa
dialogue.line: Remember who you are.

dialogue.speaker: Darth Vader
dialogue.line: I am your father.

dialogue.speaker: CNN Announcer
dialogue.line: We'll be right back.

jokes.text: Will you guys pipe down? I'm saying goodbye to Lisa. / We're sorry.
jokes.setup: Other ghosts interrupting emotional moment
jokes.punchline: They politely apologize and quiet down
jokes.explanation: Sweet follow-up to meta-joke - maintains emotional moment while being funny

dialogue.speaker: Other ghosts
dialogue.line: Sorry, sorry, we didn't realize...

dialogue.speaker: Other ghosts
dialogue.line: We'll keep it down.

dialogue.speaker: Bleeding Gums Murphy
dialogue.line: Thank you.

jokes.text: One more time! / Oh, come on, Lisa. / I got a date with Billie Holiday.
jokes.setup: Lisa wants to keep playing with ghost
jokes.punchline: Bleeding Gums has supernatural social obligations
jokes.explanation: Sweet ending that maintains the jazz theme while showing afterlife social calendar

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But we were just getting started! Can't you stay a little longer?

dialogue.speaker: Bleeding Gums Murphy
dialogue.line: I wish I could, child, but I've got a whole bunch of ghosts waiting for me at the Supernatural Musicians' Union hall. We're having a potluck.

tmdb_overview: Bart winds up in the hospital as a result of eating Krusty cereal, and when Lisa comes to visit him, she is reunited with her sax idol.
insight: Fifty jokes in 17 minutes hits peak Simpsons density; dark comedy dominates.
